INVOICING

Designing a scalable invoicing experience for enterprise users

Simplifying invoice creation, tracking, payments and financial workflows — turning thousands of fulfilled container movements into clean, GST-compliant invoices that sync straight to the books.

Smart Invoicing Module Figma · FigJam Design System Prototyping 10 weeks · 2026
Niche
B2B SaaS · Fintech · Logistics
My role
Senior UI/UX Designer
Duration
10 weeks · 2026
Tools
Figma · FigJam · Prototyping

Project

The Invoicing Module gives a B2B logistics company one place to turn a finished job into an accurate, paid invoice. It replaces a spreadsheet-and-email chain between the Operations and Accounts teams with a single shared, synchronised screen — every line container-accurate and every tax figure auto-calculated.

Challenge

Getting paid hinged on one accurate invoice — but the workflow between Operations and Accounts was a slow game of telephone, run over Excel and email. If a single container number or billing address was wrong, the whole file bounced back and the process restarted from scratch.

The real design problem wasn't showing more data. It was getting two teams with completely different needs onto the same shared record — each seeing only what their job requires, with the cost maths transparent enough to trust at a glance.

Situation

The invoicing paper trail & friction loop

When a logistics job finished, getting paid depended on one accurate invoice. But the workflow between Operations and Accounts was a slow game of telephone — typed into a spreadsheet, emailed across, re-keyed, drafted, emailed back to check, and on a single error, started all over again.

Operations
Type into Excel
DO & container numbers, by hand
→
Operations
Email the sheet
Spreadsheet sent to Accounts
→
Accounts
Re-key & draft
Copy out, add GST & surcharges
→
Accounts
Email back to check
Operations verifies the draft
REJECTED one wrong number → start over from scratch

The breakdown

One wrong field rejected the whole file. If a container number or billing address was missing or wrong, Accounts bounced it back and the process restarted — causing massive delays and a mountain of billing errors.

Two teams, two languages

The same record meant different things to each side. Operations thought in cargo — DOs, containers, addresses. Accounts thought in money — base amounts, GST, surcharges, totals. The spreadsheet forced both to wade through the other's data.

Research

Two teams, one paper trail

The whole loop ran between two teams who never needed the same information. Mapping what each actually used — and what they had to ignore — pointed straight at the fix.

Upstream · Operations Team

Operations

  • Owns the cargo facts: delivery orders, container codes, billing parties, drop-off addresses
  • Keys those details into a spreadsheet the moment a job is done
  • Every typo here becomes a rejected invoice downstream
“I just need to log the cargo once and move on.”
Downstream · Accounts Team

Accounts

  • Turns cargo lines into money: base amounts, GST, surcharges, totals
  • Re-keys everything from the emailed sheet, then drafts the invoice
  • Rejects the whole file if a single figure or reference is off
“If the maths isn't transparent, I can't sign it off.”

The friction loop, step by step

Re-key
Draft

OperationsEmail the spreadsheet to Accounts
AccountsCopy figures out, add GST & surcharges
AccountsDraft the invoice, email it back to check
BothOne error → reject & restart from scratch
Manual
Lossy handoff
Duplicated work
Slow
Back to start
Task

One screen for both teams

Kill the email-and-spreadsheet chain and replace it with a unified invoice module built on three principles.

One shared page

Both teams work from the exact same live record at the same time — no files emailed back and forth, no version drift.

No information overload

Each team sees only the data it needs to do its job — cargo for Operations, money for Accounts — and nothing it doesn't.

Crystal-clear math

Highly detailed, easy-to-read cost breakdowns stop mistakes before they happen — and before they reach the customer.

Information architecture

From unbilled request to synced invoice

One spine, two tabs: Pending Invoicing (work that owes money) and Invoices (work that's been billed). Creation is a guarded path with a preview you can't skip.

Tab 01
Pending Invoicing
Unbilled requests by billing party
→

→

Split by DO · Consolidated · Individual
→

Exact invoices, before commit
Submit
Generate

Sync
ZOHO Books

→
Deliver

PDF to the customer
INVOICED generated
PENDING TO SYNC queued
READY downloadable
ERROR retry
CANCELLED terminated
Wireframes

Structure first, in black and white

Low-fidelity wireframes locked the skeleton before any colour — proving the flow with operators while it was still cheap to change.

Invoice list
List + filters + status chips per row
Create invoice
Bill-to + charges selector + format presets
Invoice detail
Charges table + bill summary + grand total
Status & sync tracking
KPI counters + per-row sync state & retry
Action

A dual-perspective layout

Instead of two teams working in isolated silos, one responsive page splits the information by who's looking at it. Same record, same moment — two tailored views.

For Operations

The interface highlights the logistics: Delivery Order (DO) numbers, container codes, billing parties and drop-off addresses — the cargo facts they own.

For Accounts

The same record filters out the logistical clutter and surfaces the money: base amounts, tax and GST, surcharges and final totals.

The shared overview

The single screen both teams land on. Outstanding balance, invoice status and recent activity sit together, so Operations and Accounts open the same live picture instead of chasing it across spreadsheets and inboxes.

Design decisions

01 — KPI band

Four numbers finance tracks daily: net revenue, MRR, active customers, outstanding. Each shows delta vs last period — direction, not just the current value.

02 — Revenue trend

A single area chart answers “are we growing?” at a glance, with the current month pinned so the reader never has to hunt for where they are.

03 — Invoices by status

The donut maps every invoice to Paid, Pending, Overdue or Draft — the same status vocabulary used everywhere else, so the mental model never breaks.

04 — Recent activity

A timestamped feed — paid, added, failed, upgraded — makes the system's recent moves legible without opening a report.

05 — Recent invoices

The dashboard ends where the work is: a compact table of the latest invoices with amount, status pill and date. One click drops the reader into the full Invoices view — the overview is a launchpad, not a dead end.

Operations logs the cargo

Operations picks the finished jobs and confirms the cargo facts. The invoice drafts itself the moment that data is in — no spreadsheet, no email, no waiting on Accounts to start.

Step 1 — pick jobs & confirm cargo (DO, containers)
Step 2 — invoices draft automatically, ready to review

Design decisions

01 — Cargo, not data entry

Jobs arrive pre-filled with their DO numbers, container codes and billing party. Operations confirms and selects — it never re-types what the system already knows.

02 — Auto-drafted invoices

The moment cargo is confirmed, the invoice generates itself — mapping each line automatically instead of waiting on Accounts to key it in by hand.

03 — Grouped the way clients expect

Split by DO, Consolidated or Individual turns an error-prone judgement call into a labelled choice with a clear recommendation — so the invoice's shape matches the customer, not the operator's guess.

04 — Preview before commit

Exactly which invoices will be created — every line, container and subtotal — is shown before anything is final. The draft Accounts used to assemble by hand now arrives ready to check.

05 — Nothing final until it's chosen

“Modify selection” steps back without losing context, and nothing commits until submit. Because the flow handles money, it's deliberately forgiving — both teams can explore formats and totals freely, knowing the old reject-and-restart loop is gone for good.

Accounts sees the money — transparently

The same record, read for Accounts: base amounts, GST, surcharges and totals. Every line opens into a step-by-step breakdown of how the cost was calculated, so the maths can be trusted at a glance and signed off without a second spreadsheet.

Design decisions

01 — Expandable cost breakdowns

Click any line and it unrolls into a clear, step-by-step calculation — tariff × quantity, the CGST + SGST split, surcharges and subtotal. The maths is never a black box, for either team.

02 — Itemised & auditable

Every container, HSN code, tariff and tax line sits beside a bill summary and grand total — with “created by & on” always visible, so there's a clean trail behind every figure.

03 — Status anyone can read

Invoiced, Ready, Error, Cancelled — a counter at the top and a pill on every row mean both teams see exactly where each invoice stands, without asking each other.

04 — Findable at scale

Search, filters and sorting keep thousands of invoices navigable, and pagination keeps the table calm rather than endless — so finding one invoice never means scrolling for it.

Design system

Built for dense financial data

A token-driven library tuned for tables, currency and status — so a thousand invoice rows still feel calm and scannable.

Result

Faster invoicing with zero drama

Giving both teams one synchronised screen to work from transformed the workflow. The endless loops of emailing spreadsheets, rejecting files and re-typing data simply stopped — and the business got paid faster.

−46%
fewer billing disputes from final clients — automatic data mapping and transparent breakdowns made internal errors vanish
Zero
email back-and-forth — both teams log into one screen and see the same live updates instantly
Instant
invoices draft the moment Operations logs the cargo — so corporate cash flow speeds up

No more spreadsheets bouncing between inboxes, no more starting over because one container number was wrong. Two teams, one record, one source of truth — and an invoice that's right the first time.
